label: Conditional Probability
definition: The probability of one event given that another event is known to have occurred, defined as the joint probability of both divided by the probability of the conditioning event.

body.text: Conditional probability measures how the likelihood of an event changes once you learn that some other event has happened. It is written P(A given B) and defined as the joint probability of A and B together, divided by the probability of B: P(A given B) = P(A and B), divided by P(B). The definition simply restricts attention to the world where B is true and renormalizes the probabilities within that smaller world, so they once again sum to one. Conditioning is the formal act of incorporating information.

This idea is the foundation of essentially all probabilistic learning, because data arrives as information and the whole point is to condition on it. A spam filter wants the probability that a message is spam given the words it contains. A language model is defined entirely by conditional probabilities: the chance of each next token given all the tokens before it. The chain rule of probability decomposes any joint distribution into a product of conditionals, which is exactly how autoregressive models factor the probability of a whole sequence into one conditional step at a time.

Conditional probability is closely tied to independence and its absence. Two events are independent when conditioning on one tells you nothing about the other, so P(A given B) equals P(A). When that fails, the events carry information about each other, and mutual-information quantifies how much. Conditional independence, where two variables become independent once a third is known, is the structural assumption behind graphical models, naive Bayes classifiers, and many tractable approximations: it is what lets a large joint distribution factor into manageable pieces.

The asymmetry of conditioning is the source of frequent confusion and of one of probability's most important results. P(A given B) is generally not equal to P(B given A): the probability of a positive test given disease is not the probability of disease given a positive test. Bayes-theorem is precisely the rule that relates the two directions, and it is unavoidable whenever you need to invert a conditional, which in practice means whenever you reason from observed effects back to hidden causes.

More broadly, conditional probability is the bridge from prior belief to posterior belief that underlies bayesian inference, and it generalizes naturally to continuous variables through conditional densities and to expectations through the conditional expectation, the best prediction of one quantity given another. Every time a model updates what it expects in light of what it has seen, it is computing a conditional probability or an expectation derived from one.

image.alt: Venn diagram of the numbers 1 to 10 split into a prime set and an even set, with only the number 2 in the overlap of both.
image.caption: Conditioning restricts attention to a smaller world and renormalizes. Among 1 to 10 the even numbers are 2, 4, 6, 8, 10; given that a draw is even, the chance it is also prime is just one in five, since 2 is the only even prime.
